speaker: interviewer
text: Manya Feldman, tape number two. Let's pick up from where we were before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the-- the Judenrat was assigning jobs for people. What things were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Everything that was hard. They had field work in the summer. There was-- they were taking off crops from the fields, like cutting-- cutting weeds and digging up potatoes and-- and cutting corn in-- or-- or baling hay, this sort of thing. Also, in the summer, all the bridges that surrounded that little town were destroyed by the Russians when they left the area. So they started building bridges. And they needed a tremendous force of people to come and help them. So we were dragged about nine kilometers. Dragged, I mean we were walking. They walked us from the ghetto. And we had the yellow star on the front and the yellow star on the back. And we were walking, organized, but we could not walk on the sidewalks. The sidewalks were designated for people. And we were not people. We were treated like animals. We didn't have any cars in our little cities, you know, but we had horse and buggies. The horses were on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going to these-- to this kind of chores, labor. Also, we had a-- a big river flowing down the city. Our city was on a elevated little mountain. And they were building barges. So we were helping with those, I mean dragging stuff to the laborers, to the water, and the scaffolds near the bridges.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the wintertime, we used to clean the railways from the snow. And-- and if they didn't have any work, and there were many times that they didn't, they gave you a toothbrush, and you were cleaning the sidewalk in the city. This was this kind of labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living at this point? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now, the first three months, we were still in our home. And-- but we lost, already, our business, naturally. They took away, right away-- we had two cows. So they took them right away. And they created a ghetto. Our city, as I-- I told you before, it was only about 3,000 Jews. With the influx, from the Jews, from around the city, from little villages, it became probably around 5,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they designated-- they blocked off like three streets. And they pushed them in, into houses with other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lost our-- our house was outside of the ghetto. So we got into a-- a house, with two other families. There were two rooms, a big living room, one bedroom, and one kitchen. We were 11 people in these three rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it, this? At this point, what? This was '40 by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '40-- '41. They came in into '41. The ghetto was created three months later. We're talking the beginning of '42. OK? And that was the ghetto. The rations that they appropriated for the population was like 200 grams of bread for each person. But the bread was done-- was like a piece of brick, this size, because it was baked with potatoes or with other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those people, who did not have any access to any other kind of food-- and, but I mean, by access, you know, there was always black marketeering.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went out to the gate, and we met up with some gentiles, and we exchanged. Salt was a tremendous commodity. So those people who had groceries and had salt or flour, we used to exchange it with the gentiles. They used to bring us some vegetables, like potatoes, and some-- some sort of grits. And this was our diet. We only had bread. We were happy to have enough bread. We had grits, that we made like hard puddings.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no milk products. There were no meat. There was no fat, nothing, no milk. And we survived on this kind of food. And as-- when we were chased to work, they used to sort of feed us. [LAUGHS] Feed us?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a little bit of watery soup. And this is how we were supposed to work, real hard, real hard, back-breaking labor. I was, at that time, in 1942, I was 19 years old. And my brother was 16. And my sister was 21. And all three of us went to-- to work. I must relate to an incident that happened. As I said, the-- the-- the Judenrat supplied the list of the workers. And they had tremendous lists. And every day, they supplied the list to the Germans. And this is how they called us up, and they said this group is going here, and the other group is going in another place. At the beginning, there were some Jewish people that had a lot of money yet, you know, let-- left over, because they were rich, from before, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to hire-- they-- they-- they used to hire their people to go and work for them. Until, one-- one day, some-- some of these workers, or-- or natural workers, didn't show up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a shortage of workers. And I was one of them, because I worked at a thresher machine. And I had very bad eyes. And my eyes became terribly inflamed. So I couldn't. I couldn't even see. I couldn't go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they called-- they sent a note that you have to appear to the Gestapo, to the center, where the Gestapo was. Anyway, they arrested 30 girls and 22 men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in the Gestapo headquarters. We experienced horrible things, because there were other people there that they arrested. And in front of us, two Germans were standing with, you know, rubber sticks and-- and letting him run between them and just beating them until he was bleeding from every nook and cranny in his body. The parents-- or all the parents of these people, that they arrested, became terribly inflamed at the Judenrat. And they ran. And they screamed. And they stoned their homes. And they asked them to intervene, to do something. So they did. They intervened with some bribes. They gave them some jewelery. And luckily enough, they let us go. We were supposed to been-- to have been shot. So we escaped this incident. And the life in ghetto went on. We-- we were doing our holidays. The-- in the ghetto, you could move around. Nobody told you where to go. We couldn't go out, outside of the ghetto. We were standing by the gate and-- and-- and envying a cat or a-- or a horse or a chicken, anything that breathed that can-- could have freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you were standing by gate, what was the boundaries around the ghetto? Was there brick walls? Was there a wooden--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --wall? What-- what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take-- like, for instance, they took a street. And the front of the house was the-- the main street. So they boarded off the-- the windows and the doors. And you could not look out on the street. You had to go through the back. And the back was the ghetto. When you came to a break between seats-- streets, they-- they had a gate. And that gate was guarded by Jewish police and sometimes Ukrainian police. There were not too many Germans around. Jew-- Jewish police that were assigned by the Judenrat, and they were keeping order. You couldn't go-- you couldn't go in. You couldn't go out. And if you could go in, you know, our dress code was so different from the gentile population, that even without the-- the yellow sign, you know, the yellow star of David, they could've recognized us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were no-- there was no way of how to escape from that ghetto. And the-- the-- I'm sorry to say, it doesn't sound right, but it happens to be the honest truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gentile population was so inflamed against the Jews, that if it wouldn't been for their assistance, for the Germans, many more Jews would have escaped and would have been saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to go out and stand by the gate and observe the people that were going on a-- you know, strolling the streets and just-- and just thinking, what's going to happen to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had absolutely no hope. There was-- there was no relief in sight. The-- the situation was horrible. There were an awful lot of diseases that broke out in the ghetto, because people were starving from hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a lot of typhoid and-- and a lot of dysentery, you know, where they had diarrhea, you know? And-- and you-- you could see people were walking like skeletons. And some of them were lying in the streets and dying from hunger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did-- did anybody try to get out or escape out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my knowledge. I don't-- I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't-- it-- as I said, it was very dangerous. You didn't have the assistance of the outside. And let's say, if you would have escaped, they would have, right away, turned you in. I'll tell you the story of-- of when the liquidation game-- came, of how they caught the Jews, and they gave them over for a kilogram sugar or for a kilogram salt. So there was no-- no hope for Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that ghetto, I existed for a year. We didn't have any incidents of shooting in the ghetto, like in other cities, that I learned afterwards. Life was going on. We celebrated our holidays. Believe it or not, we baked our own matzoh for Pesach. The youth had schools, because all the teachers were in-- in-- in the ghetto. And we tried to live in a-- in a sane way, as much as possible, without any food. But the-- the-- the-- we-- my father was praying, I don't know to whom. But he was praying. All the Jews were praying. At that point, I felt that God had forsaken us. And in August, of 1942, on the 26th of August-- well, this-- and this date will remain forever in my mind-- we came to work that morning. And we worked. The-- the-- the work was going on in three shifts. They were changing. Every eight hours, another batch of Jews came to work. When we came-- and we came in the morning. And we were going home at 4:00 o'clock. We-- we heard that the 4 o'clock shift is not coming in. They told them not to come. So we knew that this is it. This was the end. In what fashion, we didn't know. However, when we just-- I'll-- I'll back up a little bit. When the ghetto was just created, they took us out, the whole population, to the center of the town, and they counted us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what was the count for? They needed a count for themselves. This is one thing. The other thing is to show you that you're a part of somebody. And if you dare to escape, the whole population will suffer. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they-- they-- they also demanded an awful lot from the Jewish population, in terms of-- they called it contributions. They confiscated. They wanted the Jews should dispose of all their gold that they have, all their silver, leather goods, fur, if their were any, goods dishes, good linens, good tablecloths. I mean anything. And they grab them, and they ship them all back in-- into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: At one point, they put out a-- an announcement that the ghetto, the Dabrowica ghetto, is to supply the Germans with 27 kilograms of gold. Kilograms? Now how do you get kilograms of gold? So, you know, we were so obsessed with the fact that we're going to be killed, but maybe we can buy out ourselves. So they started collecting the gold and collecting money. And they-- and they gave it to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The end came, as I said, anyway, in the-- on-- in August of-- on the 26th. [SIGHS] When I came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my mother was standing by-- [SOBBING] by the gate. And she said to me-- she was waiting for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said to me, you know, this is it. They are killing us tomorrow. And I came home. And we were just looking at each other, nothing to say. What do you say? How do you-- how do you escape all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was crying. And she said, [CRYING] she had a very hard life. Life wasn't easy to bring up five children and to work. And now, all the five children are grown up. She could relax. So look what's happening. They are taking our life away. Anyway, we-- we ate whatever there was to eat. [SOB] And my father and mother did not talk to us. They-- they were whispering to each other. And they said, you go to sleep. And we went to sleep. And [SOB] at about, I would say, midnight. It was-- I'll never forget it. It was a very bright night. The moon was shining. It was like daylight. We got a knock on the window. And a-- a neighbor of ours said to my father, you know, the ghetto is surrounded with Germans and Ukrainian police. And as the city was situated, as I said, on one side was the-- a river flowing. And the other side was very much close to the forest. So a lot of people started running out to run into the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that area was very much guarded, not to give a chance to the Jews to run. And he said, there were already casualties. They're shooting whoever is running to the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he knew that my father had an awful lot of acquaintances, in the villages, whom he did business. And he said to him, maybe you have another way that-- that we could go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, well, let's try. So he said to us, to the older kids. But we didn't know that this is already the end, that-- that-- that we are already going to be killed tomorrow. We thought, maybe it's again a counting. So he said, well, you put on a couple dresses and take the coat. It was very hot. It was in August. Take the winter coats with you. And I did not have an intention to go. Because I said, I mean, mother can't go with the two little girls. There will be terrible obstacles. We will have to climb fences. Who knows what we'll have to do? So I said, I'm staying with mother. And I said to my-- but I looked at my sister. I always looked up at her. She was older. Are you-- what are you doing? She says, I'm going. So I said, what about mother? She didn't answer. So I thought, she's going. So I went, too. And we left mother. And we went through the back doors. And we came to-- towards the gate, we were going, that-- the gate-- the-- the direction towards the river not in the other direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to a house where the-- the head of the Judenrat lived. He was the-- he was-- he had a command of the German language. And he was the liaison.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Obolsky, O-B-O-L-S-K-Y. He was-- he was a refugee from Poland. And he-- he knew the English language. So he was the liaison between the Judenrat and the Germans. If ever there was a bribe involved or any difficulties, there were always-- They always demanded something. And he-- he was relaying the information.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father knocked on his window, and he said to him, Mr. Obolsky, are you aware of the fact that the ghetto is surrounded, and-- and-- and something is terrible going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, oh, come on. Go-- go back. Go back to sleep. I'll get up in the morning, and I'll-- I'll fix it. I'll do something about it. [SOB] But my father didn't listen. We went. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- a strange thing-- thing was happening. We came to the gate, and there were no guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they knew that nobody will dare run in that direction. How could they go? The river is there, and there's a bridge. And there are an awful lot of German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got out of the ghetto. There were seven of us, us four, my father, my sister, my brother, and the neighbor, with two boys. And we were running not by the river, but in-- in-- on a street parallel to the river. And we were running. We ran out of the ghetto for about two kilometers. And we came to the shore of the river. And we found a little canoe. And we-- and we crossed the river. We almost drowned. But we crossed the river. We were all soaking wet. And we had to cross the-- a railroad. We crossed the railroad. There was nobody there. And we came into a village, where the head of the village was a-- supposedly a friend of my father's. They were-- he was doing business with us. He used to come to the city to buy merchandise. And we came to him. And we-- his was [NON-ENGLISH]. And we said, [NON-ENGLISH], my father said you have to help me out at this crucial time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't you do me a favor and send your son to Dabrowica to see what's going on? We don't understand. They ask them to-- to report to this center. I don't know what is the purpose. So he said, OK. But I can't keep you here, because Germans are coming. He was the head of the village. Why don't I take you to a place of hiding? And, in the meantime, I'll send my son. And he'll see what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far from there, from that village, was a railroad. And they'll-- that railroad was going to another city of Sarny. What they did is they, the Germans, picked up Jews, from 5 little cities, like there were 5,000 Jews-- I mean 15,000 Jews. And they brought them in-- in cars, in trains, to this city. And this is where they had prepared the graves. And they were shooting them. As we were sitting in hiding, we heard the trains running. And we didn't have to already know that-- that they were counted. We knew that something worse is happening, that they're taking them out to Sarny. Nevertheless, we waited for the-- for the-- his son. And he came in the evening. And he said, yeah, they transported all the Jews to Sarny. And that's it. When the-- there was no way back for us. Naturally, we started crying spasmodically for my mother and my two sisters and all the cousins and all the uncles and all the relatives. [SOB] And we decided to go into-- deeper into the forest. We marched about a couple kilometers. And we came to, you know, the individual houses, not a village. We came to this man, whom my father knew. He was a very, very nice man, a farmer. He lived on the farm, he and his wife and a son and a daughter-in-law and two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he saw us, he said, oh, my God, where is Sonka? That's the way he called my mother. She was such a nice person. I would've loved to have her here. Why don't you stay here? So he put us up in his barn, where he kept his livestock. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Vasill, W-A-S-I-L. I don't remember his last name. But the place where he comes from was called Hradki, H-R-A-D-K-I. And we called him, Vasill from Hradki. And he said, OK, stay here. And whatever-- well, let's see what's going to happen. Maybe you could still return back. But we knew we didn't. One night, we stayed in his barn. And at that time, since Jews started-- by the way, the shooting was going on from the trains, because Jews were jumping from the trains. And the Germans were shooting them. Some were lucky to not to caught a bullet, and some were killed right on the spot. By the same token, there were an awful lot-- not an awful lot-- quite a few gentiles that were roaming, already, the forests, for the purpose of robbing Jews. They knew that Jews escaped from the ghetto, and they had with  them some jewelery or some money. That night, as we were staying in that barn, and this-- this gentile fellow came in with a couple-- he had two girls with him-- with him. As a matter of fact, they were Jewish girls. But he-- his purpose was rob Jews. The girls didn't mean much to him. And he introduced himself. [LAUGHS] His name was Hodunko, H-O-D-U-N-K-O. He was a known robber, a known fugitive. And he said to my father, oh, nice seeing you. I'll be seeing you around. That was a-- a-- a-- a hint that we cannot-- we could not stay in this place. The-- the-- that Vasill came-- came over the next morning. He said, he's a dangerous man. You better don't face him anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he put us up about a couple kilometers from the house. My father took off his coat, and he gave it to him and a pair of boots. And he said, that's all I can give you. I don't have anything else. And you will try to feed us as much as you can. So every-- we didn't-- we were sitting just in the bushes. No-- we didn't build a bunker or anything or a hut. It was raining. It was shining, everything we had. So he used to bring us out half a bushel potatoes at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a fire. And we cooked the potatoes. And we stayed with this guy for about two weeks. And then another ghetto was disposed of. And some other Jews started running into that forest. And they came to that guy, because they were also doing business. He had a good name, a good reputation. And we saw that. We stayed another-- my cousin, by the way, also escaped. He came, and he stayed with us. And we saw that it's getting too crowded. We have to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, he couldn't feed. With the best of intentions, he couldn't feed that many people. So we decided to go deeper into the forest. For what and how and what purpose? Just to run from death.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did. We went in, deeper into the forest. And it was summer, so we built ourselves a hut just to be protected from the rain. And we stayed in that hut. And at night we used to go out in the fields and dig up some potatoes. And this is how we lived. That was August month and September. It was still nice and warm. We didn't have-- we didn't need too much clothes then. The area, there, was the-- the very well-known Polesie swamps, the Polish Polesie swamps. When you walked on it, you walked like on a pillow. And they-- the-- the earth was very un-fertile. They-- they had very little. The-- the population in that area was extremely poor. They ate bread only once-- once a year. They saved it for when they were taking off the crops. , Otherwise they lived on potatoes and cabbage and carrots, whatever they-- they could plant in their own gardens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were around that village. And we used to go, as I said, and dig up some potatoes. And-- and the-- the Jewish population in the forest grew a little bit more. We had about 50 Jews roaming from different cities. What was the object? The objective started to be to get a weapon. For what purpose? You couldn't fight the Germans, naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the weapons served served as a-- as a prevent-- not a preventive, as something that you could kill another German. You could kill somebody that threatens you. Or you keep the weapon, in case-- you kept the weapon in case the German's caught you, and you want to commit suicide. You don't want to fall in as-- as a live-- live person, because they did-- I'll tell you about an incident, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: I did have one question, before you went on, about that incident. You talked about people being turned in for sugar, for salt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. At the time when they marched them to the station, and the station was about two kilometers, Jews were running. They were running away. They-- and they-- who-- where would they ran? In the-- into the fields. Shepherds were catching them. And other gentile people were out to catch Jews. And when they turned them into the Germans, the Germans gave them some sugar and some salt as a reward. They-- they announced to them that, for every Jew that they'll bring in, there will be a reward for them.
